coredata.dc:title: Constructing broad spectrum response ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterojunction nanoplates: Synergetic mechanism of boosting redox abilities for photocatalytic degradation pollutant
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Hongquan
Sun, Jianzhe
Zang, Shuying
Gong, Lige
Feng, Lan
coredata.dc:description: Rhodium oxide quantum dots (ROQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were controllably assembled on [Bi2O2]2+ slices and [WO4]2- slices of thin layered Bi2WO6, respectively, to construct broad spectrum response ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterojunctions by a two-step process including photodeposition ROQDs and hydrothermal anchoring CQDs. 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs exhibited optimized photoactivity for 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) degradation (K app=2.15×10−2 min−1) under the visible light (420 nm﹤λ﹤800 nm) irradiation, about 9.5 times as that of pristine Bi2WO6 (K app=2.26×10−3 min−1). ROQDs-modifying played a crucial role in enhancing photoactivity under the visible light of λ>420 nm, whereas CQDs- modifying played a decisive role under the visible light of λ>550 nm. Built-in electric fields at the ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces and the intimate interfaces as charge transfer channels produced significant synergetic effects on promoting charge carrier separation and reducing charge transfer resistance. More importantly, the oxidative power of h+ was enhanced by forming Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction, thus making more contribution to 4-CP degradation by both h+ and ·OH than ·O2-. In addition, ROQDs and CQDs co-modification further improved light harvesting capacity, enlarged specific surface, pore volume, and average pore diameter, as well as increased surface hydroxyl and absorbed oxygen. Enhanced visible light photoactivity was attributed to the cooperation of all above factors. This work provides a viable avenue to develop efficient double heterojunction photocatalysts for environment purification and solar energy conversion.

Photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 4-CP over (A) 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs, (B) 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, (C) 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 with different scavengers irradiated by the visible light of 420 nm﹤λ﹤800 nm, and (D) Fluorescence spectra (excited at 332 nm) of hydroxyl radical adducts trapped by coumarin in the aqueous dispersion of Bi2WO6, 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, and 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/ 4.2%CQDs under the visible light irradiation.

(A) Mott-Schottky plots of Bi2WO6, 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6, and 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs; UPS spectra of (B) Bi2WO6, (C) 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6, (D) 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6, (E) 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs, and (F) their VB-XPS spectra.
Illustration of constructing process of ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterostructure nanoplates.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rhodium oxide quantum dots (ROQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were controllably assembled on [Bi2O2]2+ slices and [WO4]2- slices of thin layered Bi2WO6, respectively, to construct broad spectrum response ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterojunctions by a two-step process including photodeposition ROQDs and hydrothermal anchoring CQDs. 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs exhibited optimized photoactivity for 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) degradation (K app=2.15×10−2 min−1) under the visible light (420 nm﹤λ﹤800 nm) irradiation, about 9.5 times as that of pristine Bi2WO6 (K app=2.26×10−3 min−1). ROQDs-modifying played a crucial role in enhancing photoactivity under the visible light of λ>420 nm, whereas CQDs- modifying played a decisive role under the visible light of λ>550 nm. Built-in electric fields at the ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces and the intimate interfaces as charge transfer channels produced significant synergetic effects on promoting charge carrier separation and reducing charge transfer resistance. More importantly, the oxidative power of h+ was enhanced by forming Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction, thus making more contribution to 4-CP degradation by both h+ and ·OH than ·O2-. In addition, ROQDs and CQDs co-modification further improved light harvesting capacity, enlarged specific surface, pore volume, and average pore diameter, as well as increased surface hydroxyl and absorbed oxygen. Enhanced visible light photoactivity was attributed to the cooperation of all above factors. This work provides a viable avenue to develop efficient double heterojunction photocatalysts for environment purification and solar energy conversion.

Built-in electric fields at the ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces and the intimate interfaces as charge transfer channels produced significant synergetic effects on promoting charge carrier separation and reducing charge transfer resistance. The redox powers of photogenerated carriers were enhanced by constructing Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and similar n-n CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, photocatalysis has attracted intensive research interest in utilizing solar energy to solve the escalating serious environment and energy crises. To make the utmost of solar energy, developing efficient broad spectrum response photocatalysts is highly desirable, due to UV light only accounting for 4–5% of solar energy. As compared with wide bandgap semiconductors (Eg>3 eV) such as TiO2, ZnO, and KTaO3 et al., some middle bandgap semiconductors (Eg≤3 eV) have recently attracted more and more attention such as Bi3+-based photocatalysts due to suitable bandgaps [1–4]. As a typical Aurivillius oxide, Bi2WO6 has a relatively narrow bandgap (2.8 eV), appropriate band edge positions, and special layered structure stacked by alternate [Bi2O2]2+ slabs and perovskite-like [WO4]2- slabs with shared oxygen atoms to form chemical bonds, favorable to separation of charge carriers, which is particularly suitable to photocatalytic or photoelectrocatalytic applications. However, its photocatalytic application is greatly restricted by narrow light absorption (<450 nm), slow charge transfer, fast carrier recombination, and low oxidative power of photogenerated holes. Despite enormous progress in promoting photocatalytic efficiency by several effective strategies including crystal facet control, surface adjustment, heteroatom doping, defect engineering, co-catalyst loading, heterojunction constructing, and bismuth enrichment strategy, until now, overall solar energy conversion efficiency is far from commercial applications [3–7].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with ultrafine sizes below 10 nm have displayed fascinating properties for photocatalytic applications due to their conjugated π structure, wide absorption towards the solar spectrum, highly fluorescent with tunable, efficient electron transfer, robust chemical inertness, abundant, low toxicity, and inexpensive. Therefore, CQDs are rapidly boosted in activating semiconductor materials under NIR irradiation, owing to the emission of UV to visible light resulted from the transition of the π-electron in the sp2 carbon [8–11]. For example, CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials presented the full spectrum absorption, and promoted photogenerated electron transfer and surface adsorption, thus improving photocatalytic degradation pollutants [12]. However, CQDs- modifying could not enhance the redox ability of Bi2WO6. In fact, even for the most efficient up-conversion materials, their up-conversion efficiencies are no more than 20% [13]. Thus, further effort to improve the overall solar conversion efficiency is needed urgently.

Possessing sufficient thermodynamic abilities are prerequisites for boosting the redox abilities of photogenerated electrons and holes [14]. In order to further improve the oxidation ability of holes in the conduction of Bi2WO6, designing a direct Z-scheme heterojunction would make up the lack of CQDs/Bi2WO6. Noble metal oxides are promising for catalytic applications. Rh2O3 was found to be to be a effective oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalyst [15]. Rh2O3 is a p-type semiconductors [16], of which the work function (4.85 eV) is much smaller than that of Bi2WO6 (5.416 eV) [17], thus serving as an oxidative catalyst for a Z-scheme heterojunction. Considering its scarcity and cost issues, ultrathin Rh2O3 quantum dots (ROQDs) with higher surface-to-volume ratios and thus more active sites may efficiently maximize the utilization of unique surface and interfacial properties. Thus, constructing direct Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 should be feasible strategy to introduce high active hole centers, thus achieving high efficient ultilization of both photogenerated electrons and holes. To the best of our knowledge, research on ROQDs and CQDs synergetically modified Bi2WO6 three-component (3C) heterojunction system has rarely been reported. Besides size distributions and surface states of quantum dots (QDs) that significantly affect optical and electronic properties, controllable fabrication of ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs still faces conspicuous challenges such as space targeting anchoring.

Based on above consideration, broad spectrum response ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterostructure nanoplates were fabricated by photodeposition and hydrothermal processes. Modulation of the optical and electronic properties of Bi2WO6 was achieved by ROQDs or/and CQDs modification. Synergetic effects on the optical absorption, charge carrier separation and transfer, as well redox capacities were obtained by ROQDs and CQDs co-modification. Furthermore, a synergetic mechanism of enhanced photoactivity for 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) degradation was also proposed. The work provides a novel avenue to develop excellent photocatalysts for environment purification and solar energy conversion.

RhCl3(99.99%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar chemicals Ltd. Co. Bi(NO3)3.5H2O, Na2WO3.2H2O, citric acid, NaOH (97%), and Na2SO4(99.0%) were purchased from Fuhua chemicals Co. (Tianjin, PRC). All reagents were used without further purification. Distilled water was used in the whole experiments.

Bi2WO6 nanoplates were prepared by a hydrothermal method using Bi(NO3)3.5H2O and Na2WO3.2H2O as precursors and citric acid as a chelating agent. In a typical procedure, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3.5H2O was dissolved to 3.5 mL of 4 M dilute nitric acid, then 6 mmol of citric acid was added into above solution under stirring for 20 min at room temperature, obtaining solution A. 1 mmol Na2WO3.2H2O was added into 5 mL of 2 M NaOH solution, obtaining solution B. The solution B was added into the solution A, and the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7 with 1 M NaOH solution. Then, the reaction solution was transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 ℃ for 24 h. Bi2WO6 nanoplates were obtained after cooled, washed, and dried at 110 ℃ for 2 h, as well calcined at 450 ℃ for 2 h.

ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructure nanoplates were prepared by a photodeposition method using RhCl3.3H2O as a modifier. In a typical procedure, 0.1 g of as-synthesized Bi2WO6 nanoplates was dispersed in 60 mL of ethanol water (Vethanol:Vwater=1:1) by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. A certain amount of RhCl3 solution (the mass percentage of Rh3+ in Bi2WO6 nanoplates was 0.1%) was added into the above suspension under N2-purging and irradiation of a 500 W Xe lamp. ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructure nanoplates were obtained after washed several times with deionized water and dried at 110 °C, which was denoted as 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6.

In first, CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal carbonization method using citric acid and ethanediamine as precursors [18]. In a typical procedure, 1.0507 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and then 335 μL of ethanediamine was added into above solution under stirring for 1 h at room temperature. Then, the reaction solution was transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 200 ℃ for 1 h. Reddish-brown CQDs colloidal solution, which would be used directly in next experiment without further processing, was obtained after cooled to room temperature naturally.

Then, CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructure nanoplates were prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 0.1 g of as-synthesized Bi2WO6 nanoplates was dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol water (Vethanol:Vwater=1:2) by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. A certain amount of CQDs colloidal solution (the mass percentages of CQDs in Bi2WO6 nanoplates were 0.7% or 4.2%) was added into the above suspension, of which the pH was adjusted to 3 with 1 M HCl solution, and kept on stirring for 6 h at room temperature. Then, the reaction solution was transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 6 h. CQDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates were obtained after cooled, washed several times with deionized water, and dried at 110 °C, which were denoted as 0.7%CQDs/Bi2WO6 or 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6.

ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterostructure nanoplates were prepared by the hydrothermal method shown in Scheme 1. 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/0.7%CQDs or 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs nanoplates were obtained following the above synthesis procedure of 0.7%CQDs/Bi2WO6 or 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 except that Bi2WO6 nanoplates were substituted by 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was collected on a Japan Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10–90°. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a VERTEX 80 FTIR spectrometer using KBr as diluent. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and elemental mapping were carried out on a FEI Tecnai G2TF20 transmission electron microscope using an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a PHI-5400 ESCA system instrument with Al Ka radiation (hν=1486.6 eV), using the binding energy of C 1 s at 284.6 eV as a reference. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore size distributions were determined on a Quantachrome NOVA2000E specific surface area analyzer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as reference standard. Fluorescence spectroscopy of hydroxyl radical adducts trapped by coumarin was measured with an HORIBA Fluorolog spectrofluorometer at excitation wavelength of 332 nm. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was obtained with XPS instrument using He I excitation (21.2 eV) and a constant pass energy of 5 eV in the ultrahigh vacuum chamber.

Photocurrent responses, electrochemistry impedance spectra (EIS) and Mott-schottky curves were measured with a CHI660E electrochemical analyzer (Chenhua Instruments Co., Shanghai) in a three-electrode electrochemical system with a quartz window. A platinum electrode was used as counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode, and photocatalyst loaded on an FTO conducting glass as the working electrode. 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte for all measurement. A 350 W Xe lamp was served as the light source for photocurrent response measurement. Applied potential window was −1.0–1.0 V, and applied frequency was 1 kHz for the Mott-schottky curve measurement.

The photoactivities of the samples were evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation, which is similar to the reported method [19]. In this work, a 500 W Xe lamp was used as light source, the cut-off filters of 420 nm and 550 nm, as well IR-cut of 800 nm, were used to obtain the visible light with different wavelength regions. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure the establishment of an adsorption/desorption equilibrium.
The total organic carbon (TOC) contents were measured to investigate 4-CP mineralization using a Shimadzu total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCPN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, morphology, surface composition and texture properties
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, 0.7%CQD/Bi2WO6, 4.2%CQD/Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/0.7%CQD, and 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/ 4.2%CQD are presented in Fig. 1A. All samples exhibit similar diffraction peaks, the strong peaks at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.1°, and 55.9° are assigned to (131), (200), (202), and (133) crystal planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6, respectively, and the weak peaks at 35.9°, 58.6°, 68.8°, 76.0°, 78.4°, and 87.6° are corresponded to (151), (262), (400), (193), (402), and (462) crystal planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6 (JCPDS No. 39–0256), respectively [12,20], indicating that negligible effect of ROQDs and CQDs decoration on well crystallized Bi2WO6 via our modifying processes. All the CQDs-modified samples appear a very weak peak at 25.9° corresponding to (002) crystal planes of CQDs [9]. However, no other peaks from Rh impurity are observed due to its very low modifying content, much lower than XRD detection limit.

The surface groups in Bi2WO6, ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 samples were investigated by FTIR spectra in Fig. 1B. For all samples, the strong peaks at about 439 cm−1, 580 cm−1, and 731 cm−1 should be assigned to the Bi-O stretching vibrations [21], bending vibrations, and asymmetric stretching vibrations of WO6, respectively, while the shoulder peaks at about 821 cm−1 are also attributed to asymmetric stretching vibrations of WO6 [10]. The broad bands around 3442 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 are associated to the stretching vibrations of surface O-H bond and bending vibrations of surface adsorbed hydroxyl groups, respectively [10,22]. The weak peaks at 2358 cm−1 are from surface adsorbed CO2. The weak peaks at around 1484 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 are attributed to C-H scissoring and bending vibration from residual –CH3, respectively [23,24]. For all modified samples, the new peaks appear at about 1046 cm−1 corresponding to residual =C-H out of plane bending [25], while the peak at 1022 cm−1 for Bi2WO6 is assigned to C-O stretching vibration [26]. For all CQDs-modified samples, new weak peaks appear at about 2925 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1, corresponding to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of residual CH2 group, respectively [27].

The TEM and STEM images of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs reveal that the sample presents thin irregular nanoplate morphologies with many clusters less than 8 nm in size on the surface ( Fig. 2A and C). Its HRTEM image is shown in Fig. 2B, where the lattice spacings of 0.191 nm, 0.271 nm, and 0.226 nm are assigned to (220), (002), and (042) crystal planes of Bi2WO6, respectively [28]. The lattice spacing of 0.332 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [21]. By comparison with Bi2WO6 in Fig. S1, CQDs are mainly distributed on the Bi2WO6 marginal areas without agglomeration. The lattice spacing of 0.256 nm attributes to the (200) crystal plane of ROQDs, which are less than 6 nm in diameter, and evenly decorated on the layered surfaces. However, ROQDs superlattice exists as lattice order and disorder due to the very low load of 0.1%. As compared with homogeneously distributed Bi, W, and Rh elements in Fig. 2D, only a little of C element distribute on the layered surfaces, even though the CQDs-modifying content (4.2%) is much higher than the ROQDs-modifying content (0.1%), further identifying their different spatial distributions. The results confirm that both ROQDs and CQDs have been separately anchored on the Bi2WO6 surface to form intimate ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunctions via our two-step modifying processes, which will play key roles in the interface transfer of charge carriers.

To identify components, chemical states, and electronic interaction between Bi2WO6 and modified ROQDs or/and CQDs, the high resolution XPS spectra of ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 samples were presented in Fig. 3A–D. Their XPS survey spectra in Fig. S2 confirm that all samples contain the Bi, W, O, and C elements, besides of the Rh element in 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6. However, the C element in 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 should be attributed to the adsorbed carbon in the air. As shown in Fig. 3A, their Bi 4f peaks at 159.22–158.79 eV and 164.66–164.06 eV corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, are assigned to Bi3+ in all samples [21–23,29]. As compared with those of pristine Bi2WO6 (159.2 eV and 164.44 eV), 0.1%ROQDs-modifying leads to obvious negative shifts to lower binding energy about 0.41 eV and 0.38 eV for Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which should result from the photogenerated electron transfer from Rh2O3 to Bi2WO6 to equilibrate their fermi levels upon the formation of Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction, due to a lower work function (4.85 eV) of Rh2O3 [17] than that of Bi2WO6 (5.416 eV) [20]. In contrast, 4.2%CQDs-modifying causes only a slight positive shift to higher binding energy about 0.22 eV for Bi 4f5/2, which should be originated from the photogenerated electron transfer from Bi2WO6 to CQDs upon the formation of similar n-n CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction [24,30].

Moreover, their W 4f peaks at 34.7–35.5 eV and 36.95–37.38 eV corresponding to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2, respectively, are assigned to W6+ in all the samples (Fig. 3B) [23]. Similarly, 0.1%ROQDs-modifying also leads to obvious negative shift about 0.8 eV for W 4f7/2, which may be caused by producing a small amount of the lower valence W5+ [5]. The result confirms that a little of W6+ have been reduced into W5+ by photogenerated electrons accumulated on [WO4]2- slice under the photodeposition condition. In contrast, CQDs-modifying brings about only negative shift about 0.28 eV for W 4f7/2.

However, Rh element can only be detected in 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, due to its too low modifying content. As observed in Fig. 3C, Rh 3d XPS peaks appear at 309.4 eV for Rh 3d5/2 and 314.4 eV for Rh 3d3/2, confirming the existence of Rh3+ oxidation state, which exhibits a obvious positive shift in comparison to pristine Rh2O3 (308.5–308.8 eV for Rh 3d5/2, 313.2–313.6 eV for Rh 3d3/2) [15,31], well in concert with the negative shifts of Bi 4f and W 4f.

As shown in Fig. 3D, the O 1 s XPS spectra of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 are de-convoluted to three peaks at 530.1 eV, 530.7 or 531.0 eV, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to O main from Bi-O bonds in [Bi2O2]2+ slice, W-O bonds in [WO4]2- slice, and surface hydroxyl groups, respectively [21,29]. However, the O 1 s XPS spectrum of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs is fitted into four peaks at 530.5 eV, 531.0 eV, 532.5 eV, and 534.0 eV, corresponding to Bi-O, W-O, surface hydroxyl groups, and surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively [32]. As compared with those of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, the O 1 s peaks in 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs shift to higher binding energy about 0.4 eV for Bi-O and 0.3 eV for W-O, respectively, in accord with the shifts of Bi 4f and W 4f. The surface hydroxyl group content increases from 15.7% to 22.4%, and a new weak peak to adsorbed oxygen about 1.6% is observed, which will benefit to trap photogenerated holes and electrons to produce reactive species. The binding energy changes of Bi 4f, W 4f, and O 1 s for ROQDs or/ and CQDs modified samples further demonstrate that the ROQDs and CQDs have been chemically incorporated with Bi2WO6 rather than physical interaction. By comparison, it should be noted that CQDs-modifying has a greater effect on the W 4f shift than Bi 4f shift, and on the O 1 s for W-O than O 1 s for Bi-O, confirming that CQDs have a stronger impact on the chemical environment of [WO4]2- slice than that of [Bi2O2]2+ slice, thus inferring CQDs anchoring on [WO4]2- slice rather than [Bi2O2]2+ slice. The result is well in accordance to TEM analysis.

The specific surface areas and pore size distributions of Bi2WO6, ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 samples are presented in Fig. 4A and B. As shown in Fig. 4A, all samples exhibit typical type III isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops in the almost overall pressure region, implying the coexistence of uneven micropores, slit-shaped mesopores (0.05<P/P0<0.9) and open macropores (P/P0>0.9) with an interconnected pore system, which originates from both the micropores of material itself and the aggregation of QDs-anchored Bi2WO6 nanoplates [19,33]. After decorating 0.1%ROQDs and 4.2%CQDs on the Bi2WO6 surface, the micropores and mesopores with pore diameter less than 10 nm increase significantly (Fig. 4B), leading to an obvious increase in the specific surface, pore volume, and average pore diameter from 17.3 m2 g−1, 0.063 cm3 g−1, and 2.06 nm of Bi2WO6 to 42.39 m2 g−1, 0.134 cm3 g−1, and 2.32 nm, respectively, which provide more activity sites and mass transfer channels for reactive species and target pollutants, thus endowing it with remarkably improved photoactivity.

The UV–vis DRS of Bi2WO6, ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 samples are illustrated in Fig. 5A. The strong absorption of pristine Bi2WO6 in the UV–vis region less than about 432 nm is related to the electronic transition from the valence band (VB) hybridized mainly by O 2p and Bi 6s orbitals to the conduction band (CB) from W 5d orbitals of Bi2WO6 (~2.87 eV) [6,34]. As compared with pristine Bi2WO6, ROQDs or/and CQDs modifying significantly increase the light harvesting capacities in both overall visible light region and the UV region less than 337 nm. For CQDs-modified Bi2WO6, the visible light intensity enhances with increasing CQDs-modifying content from 0.7% to 4.2%, and a new broad absorption peak appears at 467 nm, resulting from the visible light response of CQDs. Upon ROQDs coupling with Bi2WO6, improved visible light absorption is attributed to the small bandgap energy for ultrathin Rh2O3 (1.6–1.7 eV) [31]. Very interestingly, ROQDs and CQDs dual-modified Bi2WO6 samples exhibit much higher visible light absorption capacities than corresponding single-modified samples, confirming that a synergetic effect of ROQDs and CQDs dual-modifying on the visible light absorption of Bi2WO6 has been produced in this work. The band gap energies of Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs estimated by Tauc approach are 2.87 eV, 2.81 eV, 2.75 eV, and 2.65 eV, respectively (Fig. 5B). The obtained band gap of pristine Bi2WO6 is the same as reported value [6,34]. Clearly, 0.1%ROQDs or 4.2%CQDs modifying decreases the band gap of Bi2WO6, and 0.1%ROQDs and 4.2%CQDs dual-modifying produces a synergetic effect on further decreasing the band gap. As a result, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQD presents the highest light harvesting capacity among them, thus benefiting to higher solar ultilization efficiency.

To explore the carrier separation and interfacial charge transfer resistance modulated by ROQDs or/and CQDs modifying, the photocurrent behaviors and EIS measurements of Bi2WO6, ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 were tested and illustrated in Fig. 6A and B, respectively. As compared with pristine Bi2WO6, ROQDs or CQDs modifying significantly enhances the photocurrent response and reduces the semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plot, confirming that the formation of ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunctions significantly promote the charge separation and interface transfer. The promotion action strengthens with increasing the CQDs-modifying content from 0.7% to 4.2%. What’s more, synergetic effects are occurred by ROQDs and CQDs dual-modifying to form ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterojunctions. Thus, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs exhibits the highest charge separation capacity and the smallest charge transfer resistance among all samples, which should attributed to built-in electric field in the Rh2O3/Bi2WO6 interfaces and CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces, as well as the intimate interfaces as charge transfer channels [35].

To explore roles of ROQDs and CQDs in improving the photocatalytic performance of Bi2WO6, their single photoactivities were tested using 4-CP as target pollutant under 500 W Xenon lamp with different cut-off filters including 420 nm, 550 nm, and 800 nm. As-prepared samples exhibit very weak adsorption ability for removal 4-CP (Table S1), however, their adsorption abilities increase slightly with modifying amount, which are favor for increasing the photoactivities. As shown in Fig. 7A, their single photoactivities for 4-CP degradation under the visible light of 420 nm <λ<800 nm increase in the following order: Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6<0.7%CQDs/Bi2WO6<4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/0.7%CQDs <0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs, indicating the existence of remarkable synergetic effect by ROQDs and CQDs decorating on the Bi2WO6 surfaces. The photocatalytic degradation reactions follow pseudo-first-order kinetics equation: -ln(Ct/C0)=Kappt, where Ct, C0, t, and Kapp are the 4-CP concentration at time t (mg L−1), initial 4-CP concentration (mg L−1), reaction time (min), and apparent pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1), respectively. As a result, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs exhibits the highest photoactivity for 4-CP degradation (Kapp=2.15×10−2 min−1), about 9.5 times as that of Bi2WO6 (Kapp=2.26×10−3 min−1).

After irradiated by the visible light of 550 nm <λ<800 nm for 120 min, however, almost no 4-CP degradation is observed over pristine Bi2WO6, while ROQDs or/and CQDs modified Bi2WO6 samples present certain photoactivities for 4-CP degradation in the following order: Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6<0.7%CQDs/Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/0.7%CQDs <4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs (Fig. 7B). Synergetic effects of ROQDs and CQDs dual-modifying are very obvious, and the removal rate of 4-CP reaches to 19.83% under the visible light irradiation of 550 nm <λ<800 nm for 120 min. By comparison, it is worth noting that 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 exhibits a much higher photoactivity than 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/0.7%CQDs under the visible light irradiation of 550 nm <λ<800 nm, while opposite result is obtained under the visible light irradiation of 420 nm <λ<800 nm, further identifying that the CQDs-modifying plays a decisive role in improving the photoactivity driven by the visible light of 550 nm <λ<800 nm, whereas the ROQDs-modifying plays a crucial role in enhancing photoactivity driven by the visible light of 420 nm <λ<800 nm.

After irradiation under the visible light of 420 nm <λ<800 nm for 120 min, the removal rate of TOC reaches 78.6% (Fig. 7C), lower than the removal rate of 4-CP (92.0%), suggesting that TOC can be effectively removed by visible light driven 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs photocatalysis, and a series of intermediates are generated in the 4-CP degradation process, which would be mineralized completely by further extending the irradiation time. In addition, the photocatalytic stability of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs was carried out for five runs under the visible light irradiation of 420 nm <λ<800 nm. As shown in Fig. 7D, the removal rate of 4-CP still reaches to 86.0% (93.5% remaining) even through five runs, and no obvious changes are observed in the XPS survey spectra of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs before and after using (Fig. S3), confirming that obtained photocatalyst possesses both high photocatalytic activity and excellent stability, which is very suitable for long time application for organic pollutant degradation.

To identify the main oxidative species in the photocatalytic degradation process, additional scavenging experiments were carried out for 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, and 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 with different scavengers under the visible light (420 nm﹤λ﹤800 nm) irradiation. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), methanol, and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), were used to scavenge the photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and superoxide radical anions (·O2 -), respectively. Moreover, N2-purging was also used to degas dissolved oxygen that would capture the photogenerated electrons (e-). As observed in Fig. 8A, under the visible light (420 nm﹤λ﹤800 nm) irradiation for 120 min, the photocatalytic removal rates of 4-CP by 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs decrease from 92.0% for no scavenger to 66.6% for addition BQ, to 56.5% for addition methanol, to 36.1% for addition EDTA-2Na, and to 45% for N2-purging, respectively. It is very nice to see that the oxidative species contribution decrease in the order of h+>·OH>·O2 -, confirming that both h+ and OH make more contribution to 4-CP degradation than ·O2 -. Specially, the 4-CP removal rate decreases to 56.5% upon addition methanol (·OH scavenger), which should result from enhanced oxidation capability of photogenerated holes by the formation of Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6, achieving our prospective target. On the other hand, the photogenerated electron also plays a very important role in the current photocatalytic system, which can be captured by dissolved oxygen in the solution to form O2 -, then immediately react with H+ to form H2O2, and finally form ·OH [36].

In order to further identify the action of ROQDs and CQDs decoration, the scavenging experiments over 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 are presented in Fig. 8B and C. For 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, photogenerated hole is vital to 4-CP degradation, while photogenerated electron has only a marginal effect on the 4-CP removal. Conversely, for 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6, photogenerated hole has little or no effect on 4-CP degradation, while ·OH is the most important oxidative species, which results mainly from photogenerated electron based on the N2-purging experiment result (Fig. 8C). To demonstrate the ·OH formation in above aqueous dispersions, the fluorescence spectra were measured using coumarin as a probe molecule. As shown in Fig. 8D, their florescence intensities increase in the order of Bi2WO6<4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6<0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs <0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6. After modified by 4.2%CQDs, a new shoulder peak at about 450 nm originated from CQDs, which disappeared upon CQDs and ROQDs co-modifying, implying the change of electronic structure and transfer characteristics. The above results further confirm that ROQDs decoration on Bi2WO6 surface boosts the oxidation capacity of the photogenerated holes, while CQDs decoration on Bi2WO6 surface enhances the reduction capability of the photogenerated electrons.

To investigate the variation of the conduction and valence band position by the ROQDs and CQDs modifications, the conduction band minimum (CBM) of Bi2WO6, 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6, 4.2%CQDs/ Bi2WO6, and 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs were investigated by Mott-Schottky plots shown in Fig. 9A. As observed, all samples present positive slopes in their liner Mott-Schottky regions, which are the conductive characteristics of n-type semiconductors. Their flat-band (FB) potential positions become lower in the order of Bi2WO6 (−0.94 V), 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs (−0.89 V), 4.2%CQDs/Bi2WO6 (−0.87 V), and 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 (−0.82 V), without further correction on base of their isoelectric point pH values. Generally speaking, the CBM of n-type semiconductor is more negative by ca. 0.2 V than the FB potential [37]. In addition, their work functions (ϕ) and Fermi energy levels (EF) were determined by the UPS spectra in Fig. 9B–E. Their EF positions estimated on base of linear extrapolation are shown in the inset of Fig. 9B-E, which are in line with the above Mott-Schottky measurements. Their ϕ values calculated from the following equation: ϕ=hv–Ecut+EF, where Ecut is the cutoff region, and hv (21.22 eV) is incident photon energy from the He I source [38,39]. It is obtained that the work function of pristine Bi2WO6 is 5.73 eV, much higher than that of Rh2O5 (4.85 eV), thus forming a Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction. The distances from their valence band maximum (VBM) to EF are obtained from the linear intersection in the VB-XPS spectra (Fig. 9F). So their VBM levels (EVB) can be calculated as shown in Table S2, and their CBM levels (ECB) are estimated from the equation ECB=EVB-Eg, Eg values are obtained by Tauc approach in above UV–vis analysis [40]. In contrast to that of Bi2WO6 (2.37 eV), the VBM of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6 is shifted to 3.2 eV due to the formation of Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction. Upon constructing 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs heterojunctions, its VBM was tuned to 3.33 eV by VB of ROQDs at Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 interface, while its CBM was modulated to 0.68 eV by CQDs HOMO at similar n-n Bi2WO6/CQDs interface.

On the basis of above analytic results, the probable mechanism is illustrated as follows. As presented in Scheme 2A, ROQDs and CQDs were controllably assembled on [Bi2O2]2+ slices and [WO4]2- slices of thin layered Bi2WO6, respectively, to construct ROQDs/Bi2WO6/CQDs heterojunctions including Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction and similar n-n CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction. Upon visible light irradiation, electrons are excited from the VB to the CB of semiconductors, the electrons in the CB of ROQDs can be driven to the VB of Bi2WO6 by the biult-in fields at the Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces [41], and the electrons in the CB of Bi2WO6 are drived to move to the CQDs HOMO by the biult-in fields at the CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces (Scheme 2B) [12], as confirmed by the XPS analytic results. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes accumulate at the ROQDs surface, whereas photogenerated electrons accumulate at the CQDs surface, thus significantly promoting the fast transfer and efficient separation of the photogenerated charge carriers. Then, the photogenerated electrons in the HOMO of CQDs are easily transferred into a excited state LUMO due to the intramolecular π-π* transition. Because the LUMO of CQDs is more negative than E° (O2/·O2 -)(−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [42], electrons at the CQDs surface can reduce surface adsorbed O2 to generate ·O2 - via one-electron reducing reaction, then immediately react with H+ to generate H2O2, and finally form ·OH. The VBM of ROQDs (3.33 eV) is more positive than the generation ·OH radicals E° (·OH/OH-) (2.38 eV vs. NHE) [21,24], thus holes are easy to react with OH- or H2O to yield ·OH radicals [36]. In contrast, the VBM of Bi2WO6 (2.37 eV) is slightly more negative than E° (·OH/OH-) (2.38 eV vs. NHE), implying that holes in its VB cannot directly oxidize OH- or H2O adsorbed on the surface to form ·OH, while the CBM of Bi2WO6 (−0.49 eV) is much negative than E° (O2/·O2 -)(−0.046 eV vs. NHE), thus electrons in its CB can reduce O2 to form ·O2 -, which can immediately react with H+ to generate H2O2, and finally form ·OH, as confirmed in Fig. 8D. Based on the scavenging experiments, for 0.1%ROQDs/ Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs, both h+ and ·OH make more contribution to 4-CP degradation than ·O2 -. As a result, both photogenerated holes at ROQDs and photogenerated electrons at CQDs contribute to the photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP into CO2 and H2O, significantly enhancing the photoactivity of 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs. Thus, it is speculated that this photocatalyst can be also effect on other pollutants.

Broad spectrum response 0.1%ROQDs/Bi2WO6/4.2%CQDs heterostructure nanoplates were fabricated by the two-step process including photodeposition Rh3+ and hydrothermal treatment CQDs, which exhibits the highest photoactivity for 4-CP degradation (Kapp=2.15×10−2 min−1) under the visible light (420 nm﹤λ﹤780 nm) irradiation, about 9.5 times as that of Bi2WO6 (Kapp=2.26×10−3 min−1). The ROQDs-modifying plays a crucial role in enhancing photoactivity driven by the visible light of λ>420 nm, whereas the CQDs-modifying plays a decisive role in improving the photoactivity driven by the visible light of λ>550 nm, due to the up-conversion property.

Built-in electric fields at the ROQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6 interfaces, and the intimate interfaces as charge transfer channels, result in the highest charge separation capacity and the smallest charge transfer resistance among all samples. More importantly, the oxidative power of photogenerated holes was enhanced by construction of Z-scheme ROQDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction, thus making more contribution to 4-CP degradation by both h+ and ·OH rather than ·O2 -.

The enhanced photocatalytic performance for 4-CP degradation under visible light can be attributed to a synergetic effect of following factors from ROQDs and CQDs co-modification, including significantly improved light harvesting capacity, promoted charge carrier separation, reduced charge transfer resistance, enhanced redox powers of photogenerated carriers, obvious increased specific surface, pore volume, and average pore diameter, as well as increased surface hydroxyl and absorbed oxygen. A novel avenue to develop excellent photocatalysts for environment purification and solar energy conversion was provided in this work.

Hongquan Jiang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing -review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jianzhe Sun: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Shuying Zang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lige Gong: Software. Lan Feng: Validation.
